content.text: Claude is available through four distinct deployment paths: the Direct Anthropic API, Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Vertex AI, and Microsoft Azure AI Foundry. Each path uses the same underlying Claude model but differs in where the inference runs, how you authenticate, how data is governed, and what cloud services are colocated.

Choosing the wrong path is not catastrophic — migrating between paths requires only changing the client constructor and credentials. But making the right choice upfront avoids compliance review re-work, data migration headaches, and latency surprises caused by cross-cloud hops.
where does the rest of your application already live? Claude should sit in the same cloud boundary as your data and services to minimize latency, simplify compliance, and consolidate billing.

attrs.alt: Claude deployment platform comparison matrix covering setup complexity, auth, data residency, multi-region, HIPAA, batch, model availability, ecosystem, and private networking

content.text: Use this decision sequence to pick a platform:
Does your organization mandate a specific cloud provider?
If yes, that provider wins regardless of feature parity. Most enterprises have a preferred-provider policy that controls security review, compliance audits, and procurement.
Do you need contractual data residency?
If yes, eliminate the Direct API (Anthropic-US only). Choose Bedrock, Vertex AI, or Foundry based on which provider's DPA you already have signed.
Are you in a regulated industry (HIPAA, FedRAMP, PCI)?

All three cloud providers have relevant certifications. Confirm your specific service is covered (e.g. Bedrock is on AWS's HIPAA eligible services list; Vertex AI is on Google's). The Direct API supports HIPAA only at the Enterprise tier.
Is this a prototype or production system?
Prototypes should start on the Direct API — lowest friction. Production systems should use the cloud provider that matches your existing infrastructure.
What does your data stack use?

Colocate Claude with your data. If your docs are in S3 and your pipeline runs on Lambda, Bedrock avoids egress costs and cross-cloud latency. If your data is in BigQuery, Vertex AI is the natural fit.

The Direct API gets new Claude models first — typically on the release date. Cloud provider platforms (Bedrock, Vertex AI, Foundry) receive the same models after a lag that can range from days to weeks, depending on the model and provider. If you need day-one access to new capabilities, the Direct API has an advantage.

However, cloud provider platforms may offer managed features not available on the Direct API at all: Bedrock Guardrails, Bedrock Knowledge Bases (RAG), Vertex AI Search, and Azure AI Search are cloud-native services that combine with Claude in ways that require the managed platform.
Per-token pricing for Claude is the same across all four platforms for the same model. What differs is:
Cloud provider egress rates (~$0.09/GB out to internet)

For most applications, egress and monitoring costs are negligible compared to token costs. The exception is high-volume document processing where large documents move between services — colocating Claude and your storage eliminates cross-cloud egress.
The Direct API requires one environment variable (

) and zero cloud setup. A Bedrock or Vertex AI deployment requires: cloud account setup, service enablement, IAM role creation and policy attachment, (optionally) VPC endpoint configuration, and cloud-specific monitoring setup. This is a one-time setup cost, but it is real and can take hours to days in an enterprise with change management processes.

Start with the Direct API for your first prototype. Only switch to a cloud provider when you have a concrete compliance, data residency, or ecosystem integration requirement. Migration takes less than a day — changing the client constructor and updating credentials.

Bedrock's cross-region inference profiles spread load across multiple AWS regions within a geography automatically, giving the highest effective throughput ceiling among the four options. The Direct API has a single global quota pool. Vertex AI and Foundry require you to distribute across regions manually or accept a single-region quota ceiling.

In practice, throughput limits are rarely the deciding factor for most applications. They matter for high-volume batch pipelines (thousands of requests per minute) or latency-sensitive real-time applications with variable traffic spikes.
Fastest setup; no cloud account needed
Colocated with EC2/Lambda/S3; single AWS bill
Colocated with BigQuery/GCS; single GCP bill
Entra ID auth; Azure OpenAI + Claude in one place
Direct API for non-sensitive; cloud provider for regulated data
Bedrock or Vertex AI or Foundry
BAA available; data stays in compliant region
Data stays in EU geography under EU DPA
Bedrock Batch or Direct Batch API

Migrating from one platform to another requires changing three things: the client constructor, the authentication mechanism, and the model ID format. Business logic, prompt engineering, and conversation history management stay the same.

# Direct API
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-ant-...")
model = "claude-opus-4-8"

# Amazon Bedrock
import anthropic
client = anthropic.AnthropicBedrock(aws_region="us-east-1")
model = "anthropic.claude-opus-4-8"

# Google Vertex AI
import anthropic
client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(region="us-central1", project_id="my-project")
model = "claude-opus-4-8@20251101"

# Azure AI Foundry
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic(
    api_key="YOUR_FOUNDRY_KEY",
    base_url="https://YOUR_ENDPOINT.openai.azure.com/",
)
model = "claude-opus-4-8"

# Everything else stays identical:
response = client.messages.create(
    model=model,
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello!"}],
)
print(response.content[0].text)

This means you can prototype on the Direct API, prove out the use case, and then migrate to your cloud provider's platform with a one-afternoon refactor. Keep authentication details in environment variables from day one — that is the only code change you need.
Common Mistakes When Choosing a Platform
Starting with a cloud provider for a prototype
: IAM, VPC, and compliance setup add hours of overhead before you write any application code. Use the Direct API first.
: If your documents live in S3 and you call the Direct API from Lambda, each request pays AWS-to-internet egress. Use Bedrock instead — calls stay in-network and egress cost disappears.
: Your Direct API quota and your Bedrock quota are separate. Exhausting one does not affect the other — but you cannot combine them either.
Not pinning model versions on Vertex AI
) to prevent silent upgrades. Forgetting this means your application may silently switch behavior when a new version deploys.
: Multi-region failover adds significant complexity. Start single-region; add HA only when you have measured downtime impact exceeding your SLA.
Each platform has a different security model, and the differences matter for threat modeling and penetration testing scope.

The Direct API uses a long-lived API key stored in your environment. The threat model is: if the API key leaks (via a code repo, log file, or compromised CI system), an attacker can make unlimited calls and incur charges. Mitigate by: rotating keys regularly, using secrets management (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager), and setting spend limits in the Anthropic console.

Bedrock uses AWS IAM. There are no long-lived API keys for Bedrock itself — instead, a compromised IAM role credential (which is short-lived by design on EC2/Lambda/ECS) can call Bedrock. The blast radius is limited to the
actions on the specific model ARNs in the policy. Rotate IAM access keys for users; never create long-lived credentials for service roles.

Vertex AI uses GCP service account credentials. Service account key files (JSON) are long-lived by default — treat them like passwords. On GCP compute (GKE, Cloud Run, Compute Engine), use Workload Identity instead: credentials are short-lived tokens managed by the platform. A compromised Workload Identity token expires within one hour.

Azure AI Foundry supports Managed Identity — the Azure equivalent of AWS EC2 Instance Profiles. On Azure-hosted compute, the managed identity token is obtained and refreshed automatically with no credentials stored in your application. For local development, Entra ID personal tokens expire in 1–24 hours depending on your tenant's policy.
Use Secrets Manager; set spend limit
Use task roles; minimal IAM policy
Service account key OR Workload Identity
Use Managed Identity on Azure compute
Where do logs go when something goes wrong? The answer differs by platform and has practical implications for your on-call runbook.
Enable model invocation logging in Bedrock console
Enable request/response logging in Vertex AI settings
Enable diagnostic settings on AI Foundry resource
For the Direct API, you must build your own logging layer because Anthropic does not expose request logs to customers. Wrap your
calls with structured logging that captures model, input token count, output token count, latency, and (if safe to log) a truncated version of the prompt and response.

import anthropic, time, logging, json

logger = logging.getLogger("claude")
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def logged_call(messages, model="claude-opus-4-8", max_tokens=1024, **kwargs):
    start = time.perf_counter()
    try:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            messages=messages,
            **kwargs,
        )
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        logger.info(json.dumps({
            "model": model,
            "input_tokens": response.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": response.usage.output_tokens,
            "latency_s": round(elapsed, 3),
            "stop_reason": response.stop_reason,
        }))
        return response
    except Exception as e:
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        logger.error(json.dumps({
            "model": model,
            "error": str(e),
            "latency_s": round(elapsed, 3),
        }))
        raise
Network latency is additive to model inference time (TTFT — time to first token). The baseline latency depends on where your application server is relative to Claude's inference infrastructure.
Routing overhead; worth it for throughput
Vertex AI (same GCP region as app)
Cloud boundary crossing; avoid for latency-sensitive apps

: running your application on AWS but calling Vertex AI (or vice versa) forces traffic across the public internet (or peering points) between cloud providers. This can add 50–150ms to every request. Always colocate your application server and Claude inference in the same cloud provider when latency matters.
Before committing to a platform for a regulated application, verify the following with your compliance team:
: Does the platform commit in writing that inference requests and responses stay in the region you specify? (Bedrock, Vertex AI, and Foundry: yes with DPA. Direct API: not for non-enterprise tiers.)

: Is a Business Associate Agreement available and does it cover the Claude inference service specifically? (Bedrock: AWS BAA covers it. Vertex AI: Google BAA covers it. Foundry: Microsoft BAA covers it.)
: Request the current SOC 2 Type II report from the provider's trust portal. Verify scope includes the AI inference services.
Encryption at rest and in transit
: Confirm TLS 1.2+ in transit and AES-256 at rest for all regions you will use. All four platforms satisfy this.
: Confirm that API calls are logged with timestamps and identity information, and that logs are retained for the period required by your compliance framework.
: Some frameworks (PCI DSS, FedRAMP) require the right to audit third-party processors. Cloud provider BAAs typically include this.
: Confirm that your API requests are not used to train future models without consent. All four platforms have opt-out mechanisms at the enterprise tier.

Some organizations adopt a hybrid: the Direct API or one cloud provider for non-sensitive workloads, and a second cloud provider for regulated data. A common pattern is using the Direct API for internal developer tools and chatbots (no PII), while routing customer-facing pipelines that process PII through Bedrock or Vertex AI.
This pattern routes traffic to the right compliance boundary at the code level. The application is data-classification-aware without the operator having to remember which endpoint to call.

The four Claude deployment paths differ primarily in where your data is processed, how you authenticate, and what cloud services you can collocate. The Direct API is the lowest-friction starting point for prototyping. Amazon Bedrock is ideal for AWS-hosted applications and provides the most mature multi-region inference story. Google Cloud Vertex AI is the natural choice for GCP-centric organizations with data in BigQuery or Cloud Storage. Microsoft Azure AI Foundry fits Microsoft-first organizations that use Entra ID and Azure compliance frameworks. Per-token pricing is identical across platforms — choose based on your compliance requirements, existing cloud provider relationships, and where your data already lives.
